coredata.dc:title: Selective localization of carbonized polymer dots in amorphous phase towards high breakdown strength and energy density of PVDF-based dielectric composites

coredata.dc:description:
In a general way, there is a contradictory between dielectric constant (
ε r
) and breakdown strength (
E b
) in dielectric materials, and improving the discharge energy density (
U d
) of dielectric polymers has become a great challenge. The semicrystalline ferroelectric polymer polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is favored for its high
ε r
, but its relatively weak
E b
in amorphous regions makes it still difficult to obtain appreciable
U d
. In response to the fact that the current method of introducing rigid chain amorphous polymers into the amorphous regions of PVDF has limited capability to enhance its
E b
, in this work, due to the crystallization induced phase separation, carbonated polymer dots (CPDs) as well as PMMA were introduced into the amorphous region of PVDF, and CPDs/PVDF/PMMA composites were prepared towards high
E b
and
U d
. It is confirmed that, CPDs significantly increase the entanglement density of molecular chains in amorphous regions of PVDF; in addition, CPDs rely on their inorganic carbon cores with unique electrical properties to resist carrier migration in amorphous regions of PVDF under high electric fields. In brief, CPDs are used as a reinforcing agent for the amorphous region of PVDF to further enhance its
E b
and
U d
. The composite loaded with 0.1 wt% CPDs exhibits the superior
U d
of 12.4 J/cm3 at the
E b
of 652.0 MV/m. This work provides new understanding on the dielectric response of ultrasmall-sized CPDs on polymer dielectrics, which could help us design new dielectric polymer composites with suppressed segmental motions for high breakdown strength and high energy density applications.

D m
−

of the neat PVDF/PMMA blend and the composites; (d) Energy efficiency and discharge energy density under the external electric fields.

head.ce:title: Selective localization of carbonized polymer dots in amorphous phase towards high breakdown strength and energy density of PVDF-based dielectric composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Technologies of Materials (Ministry of Education), Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu, 610031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In a general way, there is a contradictory between dielectric constant (
ε r
) and breakdown strength (
E b
) in dielectric materials, and improving the discharge energy density (
U d
) of dielectric polymers has become a great challenge. The semicrystalline ferroelectric polymer polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is favored for its high
ε r
, but its relatively weak
E b
in amorphous regions makes it still difficult to obtain appreciable
U d
. In response to the fact that the current method of introducing rigid chain amorphous polymers into the amorphous regions of PVDF has limited capability to enhance its
E b
, in this work, due to the crystallization induced phase separation, carbonated polymer dots (CPDs) as well as PMMA were introduced into the amorphous region of PVDF, and CPDs/PVDF/PMMA composites were prepared towards high
E b
and
U d
. It is confirmed that, CPDs significantly increase the entanglement density of molecular chains in amorphous regions of PVDF; in addition, CPDs rely on their inorganic carbon cores with unique electrical properties to resist carrier migration in amorphous regions of PVDF under high electric fields. In brief, CPDs are used as a reinforcing agent for the amorphous region of PVDF to further enhance its
E b
and
U d
. The composite loaded with 0.1 wt% CPDs exhibits the superior
U d
of 12.4 J/cm3 at the
E b
of 652.0 MV/m. This work provides new understanding on the dielectric response of ultrasmall-sized CPDs on polymer dielectrics, which could help us design new dielectric polymer composites with suppressed segmental motions for high breakdown strength and high energy density applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to supercapacitors and chemical fuel cells, dielectric capacitors have the highest power density (∼108 W/kg) and are widely applied in advanced electronic and electrical systems, such as hybrid electric vehicles (HEV), high voltage direct current transmission (HVDC), etc [1–3]. With the development of flexible electronics, polymer-based dielectrics are considered as potential candidates for the preparation of dielectric capacitors with excellent energy storage properties due to their inherent low loss, flexibility, light weight, and easy processability [4–6].

The energy density (
U
) of polymer dielectrics is defined as
U = ∫ E d D = ∫
ε 0
ε r
E d E
, where
ε 0
is the vacuum dielectric constant (8.85 × 10−12 F/m),
ε r
and
E
represent the dielectric constant of polymer composites and applied electric field. Obviously, improvement of dielectric constant (
ε r
) and breakdown strength (
E b
) is the main strategy for obtaining good discharge energy density (
U d
) in polymer dielectric. Unfortunately, as the only commercially polymer dielectric, the
U d
of biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP) is limited to about 2 J/cm3 due to its low
ε r
(∼2.2) [7]. Over the past decade, a great deal of research has been placed on improving the
U d
of polymers by increasing their
ε r
, i.e., adding fillers to polymers that can contribute high
ε r
, including ferroelectric ceramics [8,9] and conductors [10,11]. Nevertheless, in most cases, there is a dielectric difference between the nanofiller and the polymer matrix [12,13], the increasing of the
ε r
is at the expense of
E b
and still cannot effectively improve the
U d
of the composites.

In contrast, recent studies have shown that wide bandgap fillers such as boron nitride nanosheet (BNNS) [14], molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) [15], and silicon carbide (SiC) [16], etc., are theoretically effective in enhancing the
E b
of the polymer matrix due to their wide bandgap and similar
ε r
to that of polymers. Recently, Shen et al. prepared the polyetherimide (PEI)-based composites containing Al2O3 nanofillers with a wide bandgap of 8.8 eV, in addition, the composites exhibited the stable
U d
and the improved energy efficiency (
η
) under 150 °C [17]. This phenomenon is attributed to the ability of Al2O3 nanofillers to suppress the injected charges from Schottky emission in the nanocomposites, thus significantly reducing the leakage current in the composites and improving the
E b
. Recently, quantum dots with block effect on electron motion have attracted great interests, because they can effectively improve the
E b
of the composites [18]. However, the lack of surface functional groups makes ultrasmall quantum dots difficult to disperse well within the polymer matrix and to produce a solid interfacial bonding, which is very detrimental to the dielectric properties of this composite material. Therefore, Wang et al. reported the incorporation of ultralow loading (<1 vol%) of low
ε r
hydroxylated Cd1-xZnxSe1-ySy quantum dots into poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (P(VDF-HFP)) [19]. Through experimental verification and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, the excellent dielectric performances of the composites are attributed to the unique structural changes including transitions in polymer chains conformation and induced interfacial dipoles induced by nanofillers. However, the surface modification of fillers could bring about extra costs or damage the intrinsic performance of fillers, which is not suitable for large-scale production of high-performance dielectric polymer composites.

As a semicrystalline ferroelectric polymer, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) possesses a high
ε r
due to the presence of intrinsic dipoles and is expected to be used in the preparation of advanced polymer dielectric composites [20]. Recently, the strategy via optimizing the intrinsic characteristic of the PVDF polymer to improve the energy density are gradually rising. The typical methods mainly focus on tailoring the ferroelectric domains with small crystallites. For example, bulky co-monomers such as clorotrifluoroethylene (CTFE), trifluoroethylene (TrFE) and hexafluoropropylene (HFP) etc. were introduced into the PVDF main chains, which break up the large ferroelectric domains into polar nanodomains, resulting in higher
ε r
and
U d
[21,22]. Nevertheless, because of the presence of molecular defects, which will inevitably reduce their mechanical strength and
E b
. It has also been reported that the electric breakdown paths tend to grow along the amorphous regions of crystalline polymers at high electric fields, which means that the
E b
of PVDF and PVDF copolymers will be limited to a low level [23]. Some studies have demonstrated that the introduction of rigid poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) molecular chains with strong interaction with PVDF can increase the molecular chain entanglement density in amorphous regions of PVDF, thus suppressing carrier transportation, improving the mechanical strength and enhancing its
E b
to some extent [24,25]. For example, Liu et al. prepared PVDF/PMMA blends and observed that PMMA was expelled between PVDF lamellae during the crystallization of the blends [26]. Due to the restriction effect of PMMA on free volume and carrier transportation in the amorphous regions of the blend, the
E b
of the blend at room temperature is about 30 % higher than that of pure PVDF. Nevertheless, the introduction of high content PMMA has insufficient capability to enhance the
E b
of PVDF, making it still difficult to obtain a considerable
U d
. Therefore, significant efforts are still needed to solve the problem of deteriorating breakdown strength of composites.

Selective localization of nanofillers in polymer blends is an effective way to tailor the performance of blends, which is decided by the thermodynamic (interaction between nanofillers and two polymer components) and kinetic effects (migragation of nanofillers). The PMMA and PVDF are regarded to be miscible regardless of the blend proportion. During PVDF crystallization process, PMMA is gradually expelled from PVDF crystallites and segregates in the interlamellar and interspherulitic region in the blending owing to the so-called “crystallization induced phase separation”. In previous work, it has been confirmed (amino or carboxyl-modification) carbon nanotubes (CNTs) exhibited relatively stronger affinity to PMMA in PVDF/PMMA blends, which indicated that PMMA chains could be adsorbed to carbon nanotubes to a greater extent than PVDF chains [27,28]. Therefore, a large amount of work has displayed that the nanofillers can be distributed targetedly in the amorphous phase between PVDF spherulites, leading to tuned dielectric and piezoresistive behaviors performance [29,30]. Carbonized polymer nanodots (CPDs) are a kind of zero-dimensional materials with size less than 10 nm. Different from the quantum dots, CPDs possess an aggregated carbonized polymer core and abundant polymer chains or groups [31]. These structural features make the CPDs exhibit unique characteristics. Firstly, the abundant groups of the CPDs makes it possible that CPDs can be easily and uniformly dispersed in the polymer matrix through interfacial bonding with polymer matrix, which is very important to the final properties of the composite. Moreover, the energy band structure of CPDs can be tailored to improve the electron affinity, thereby endowing them with high electron affinity. In this case, the CPDs can be applied to capture the injected or excited electrons via electrostatic attraction, and the large difference between the CPDs and polymer matrix could induce the formation of charge traps to prevent the trapped electrons escaping. Finally, the leakage current and conduction loss could be reduced [31]. However, the application of CPDs in the electronic or electrical fields has not been fully studied and the underlying mechanism of CPDs in the dielectric capacitors is still unclear.

Here, we prepared ultra-small (∼8.5 nm) aminated carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) and introduced the CPDs into the amorphous regions of PVDF using PMMA as the carrier. Due to the crystallization-induced phase separation of PVDF, CPDs are selectively introduced into the amorphous region of PVDF, which improves the entanglement density of the amorphous region. Meanwhile, CPDs have a good affinity for electrons and trap carriers well in amorphous region. In this case, the breakdown strength of PVDF is improved from 507.8MV/m for PVDF/PMMA blend to 652 MV/m for the composites and an increased energy storage density from 5.7 J/cm3 to 12.1 J/cm3 was obtained. These values are much higher than the 2–3 J/cm3 at 200–300MV/m typically reported for PVDF [32]. Unlike other strategies to improve the energy storage via tailoring ferroelectric domains with small crystallites or interface engineering as mentioned above, this strategy of selectively locating CPDs focus on enhancing the resistance ability to breakdown strength for amorphous phase, which is considered as weakness of semicrystalline polymer when exposed to high voltage. Herein, the expansion of the electrical tree in the amorphous region is suppressed, and the high polarization brought by the PVDF crystal phase is still preserved, finally achieving a balance between dielectric constant and breakdown strength. Obviously, this work not only demonstrates a novel approach to prepare dielectric composites with high withstand voltage, but also shows great potential on the energy storage application.

PVDF powders (
M w
= 550,000 g/mol) were purchased from Arkema. PMMA powders (
M w
= 350,000 and 150,000 g/mol, respectively) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (U.S.A.). N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.9 %) was provided by Kelong Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). All ingredients were purchased unpurified and used directly.

6 mmol citric acid (1.26 g), 18 mmol urea (1.08 g), and 30 mL deionized water were added to a hydrothermal reactor with a capacity of 50 mL, and the mixture solution was stirred evenly. Next, the hydrothermal reactor with the mixed solution was transferred to a vacuum oven, heated from room temperature and held for 6 h starting from the time when it was raised to 200 °C. After the reaction time was over, the hydrothermal reactor was cooled with the oven for 12 h. Subsequently, the solution obtained after the reaction was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min, and the upper transparent layer was collected, which was repeated at least three times. Then, the clarified solution obtained by centrifugation was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 500 Da for at least 48 h, during which uninterrupted water changes were required. Finally, the dialysis-completed clarified solution was freeze-dried for 72 h to obtain CPDs. The synthesis principle is given in Fig. 1 a.

In this work, the content of PMMA was set to 30 wt%. Firstly, a certain amount of CPDs was dispersed in the NMP by ultrasound assistance and corresponding dispersion was obtained. The CPDs dispersion was then transferred to a stirring table, PMMA powders were added to the dispersion first and stirred for 2 h, followed by adding PVDF powders to it and stirring for 24 h at 50 °C. The uniformly mixed polymer solution was vacuum defoamed for 30 min, immediately thereafter, the solution was coated to a clean glass substrate by means of a metal blade. Then, the cast film was dried at 70 °C for 12 h to evaporate the NMP. Finally, the film was kept at 200 °C for 1 h and immediately quenched in liquid nitrogen, after which the film was peeled from the glass substrate to obtain the dielectric composite film. For a comparative study, the PMMA with lower molecular weight (
M w
= 150,000 g/mol) has also been applied to prepared the CPDs/PVDF/PMMA blends through the same fabrication process.

The lattice fringes of CPDs and the dispersion of CPDs in the composites were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL). The TEM characterization used to study the films was carried out at an operating voltage of 200 kV. Before testing, CPDs dispersions need to be dropped onto a carbon support film, while the composites need to be embedded in epoxy resin and cryo-thin sectioned. The cross-sectional morphology of composite films was observed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7800F, JEOL), and it was operated at an accelerating voltage of 2.7 kV. Before the measurement, 10 nm thick gold was sputtered on the samples for SEM characterization. Dimensional study of CPDs by tap-contact mode using atomic force microscopy (AFM, Multimode 8, Bruker). Before testing, CPDs were dispersed in deionized water and sonicated, then the dispersion was dropped onto clean silicon wafers and dried for use.

The crystal structures of CPDs as well as composites were investigated by x-ray diffraction (XRD, X'pert PRO, Panalytical) and their physical phase analyses were carried out. The XRD was operated at 45 kV and 40 mA using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation and the scanning angle was from 5° to 40°. Raman spectroscope (XplorRA ONE, HORIBA) was used to define the degree of carbonation of CPDs. The wavenumber range of the Raman test was from 1000 to 2000 cm−1.The functional groups of CPDs and the crystalline phases of the composite films were probed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (FTIR, Nicolet iS20, Thermo Fisher). All experiments were performed in the wavenumber range from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The absorption spectra of CPDs and the transmittances spectra of composite films were recorded on an ultraviolet–visible spectroscope (UV-2600, SHIMADZU). All composite films were tested in the wavelength range of 200 nm–1000 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher) was carried out for further structural analysis of CPDs. The data was analyzed by Avantage software. The thermal behaviors of the composites as well as the crystallinity were investigated using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, 204 F1, Netzsch). The temperature-modulated differential scanning calorimeter (TMDSC, Q2000, TA) was utilized to record the reversing heat capacity to reveal the glass transition so as to explore the interface structure between CPDs and polymer chains.

The dielectric properties of the composite films were measured using a broadband dielectric spectrometer (BDS, Concept 41, Novocontrol). The test voltage was 1 V. Prior to the BDS test, electrodes with a diameter of 10 mm were sputtered on both surfaces of the composite film using an ion sputterometer. Electrical insulating properties of composite films studied by breakdown voltage tester (BDJC-50, Beiguang Jingyi Instrument). A gold electrode with a diameter of 4 mm was sputtered on both surfaces of the composite film prior to the breakdown strength (BD) test.

The dynamic hysteresis loops were measured using a ferroelectric test system (TF2000E, aixACCT, Germany) with a high voltage amplifier (Trek, 610E, U.S.A.) with a range of 0–10 kV at a frequency of 10 Hz. Gold electrodes with an area of 3.14 mm2 were sputtered on both sides of the composite films before all composite films were tested. The leakage current density measurement (I-E loops) was also obtained by this system. A temperature range of 10–90 °C were measured by using an electrometer. The samples were polarized at 60 °C for 30 min, then rapidly cooled down to 10 °C held for 5 min, the samples were short circuited and the current recorded as the temperature increased from 10 to 90 °C.

Fig. 1b1 and 1c1 provide the TEM and AFM images to collect the information of CPDs lattice and dimensions. As Fig. 1b2 showing, the lattice spacings of CPDs-1 and CPDs-2 are 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm, which correspond to the (002) and (100) planes of graphene, respectively [33]. The AFM amplitude diagram in Fig. 1c1 shows that CPDs have relatively uniform size and their radial size is less than 5 nm. The fitted curve was calculated by averaging the statistics of the lateral dimensions of the CPDs as shown in Fig. 1c2, and the diameter of the CPDs is about 8.5 nm. In fact, the homogeneous size of CPDs will directly affect their dispersion in the solvent and also greatly influence the unique photoluminescence phenomenon of CPDs [34]. The digital photographs in Fig. 1c2 confirm that the NMP dispersion of CPDs has good stability and appears brown under natural light while emits blue light under UV irradiation at a wavelength of 365 nm.

XRD was used to identify the crystal structure of CPDs. The typical profile in Fig. 2 a shows a dispersion peak with a 2
θ
value of 20–30°, which is the typical characteristic of disordered carbon atoms [35]. Graphite structure in CPDs is usually characterized using Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2b, there are two absorption bands near 1360 and 1950 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of CPDs, which correspond to the D-band and G-band, respectively. As a matter of fact, the D-band is interpreted as a defect in CPDs and a disordered structure of sp2, while the G-band is the E2g vibrational mode of the sp2 carbon domain [36]. Furthermore, the ratio of the D-band to the G-band absorbed intensity is defined as the degree of graphitization of the CPDs and has a value of 0.48. Therefore, it can be concluded that CPDs are composed of mostly disordered carbon with a small amount of crystalline carbon, as schematically shown in Fig. 1a. The chemical bonds and functional groups on the surface of CPDs were further characterized by FTIR, UV–vis and XPS. According to Fig. 2c, the absorption peak at 1404 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H bending vibration, while 3425 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H stretching vibration. The absorption band located at 3265-3198 cm−1 is attributed to the N–H stretching vibration; the peak at 1580 cm−1 corr esponds to the stretching vibration of C–N [37]. The band gap of CPDs is about 3.48 eV (Fig. 2e), which indicates that CPDs have the characteristics of semiconductor. To give more precise information about the functional groups on the surface of CPDs, XPS spectra were provided and the results are shown in Fig. 2f–i. Fig. 2f displays the three peaks in the XPS full spectrum at 283, 530 and 398 eV, corresponding to C1s, O1s and N1s respectively, while Fig. 2g, h, and i exhibit their respective high-resolution spectra. The three peaks of the C1s fine spectrum in Fig. 2g correspond to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO/CN, respectively. Similarly, the three peaks of the N1s fine spectrum in Fig. 2h locate at 399.8, 400.4 and 401.4 eV, which correspond to the C–N–C, N-(C)3 and N–H [38], respectively. In addition, the presence of the N-(C)3 peak also indicates that CPDs guarantee a certain degree of carbonation [39]. The two peaks in the fine spectrum of O1s in Fig. 2i correspond to CO and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively. Therefore, the unique core-shell structure of CPDs has been confirmed, in which the carbon core was surrounded by the shell containing polar groups.

The microscopic morphology of PVDF/PMMA blend composites was investigated using scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy. According to Fig. 3 a, the polymer-based CPDs composite films possess good transparency in the visible band. Specifically, the transmittance at the wavelength of 600 nm of the PVDF/PMMA blend is 95.4 %, 93.4 %, 92.1 %, 91.8 %, 90.0 % and 88.8 % for the composites containing 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 wt% of CPDs, respectively. In addition, composites loaded with CPDs exhibit a certain degree of UV shielding properties in the UV band. Considering the overall homogeneity of the composite film, the digital photograph of the film with the CPDs loading of 0.1 wt% and the size of 6 cm × 4 cm are also provided in Fig. 3a. It is not difficult to find that CPDs are uniformly dispersed in the blend matrix without significant agglomeration, which facilitates the large-scale fabrication of transparent dielectric films. According to the SEM images of the composites with different CPDs loadings (Fig. 3b–f), it is confirmed that the introduction of CPDs do not produce obvious defects in the composite with compact structure. As the TEM image of the composite containing 0.1 wt% CPDs shown in Fig. 3g, the overall distribution is relatively uniform but with a small amount of strip-like aggregation in local areas, and selective location of CPDs with PMMA in amorphous region would be reasonable for the local aggregation. Fig. 3h with the local magnification for the strip-like aggregation indicates that CPDs do not contact each other. Then, in the Fig. 3i of the local amplification of one CPD, the lattice diffraction stripe can be clearly observed.

The microstructures of polymer-based CPDs composites films were investigated by FTIR, XRD and DSC. The chain conformation of PVDF in composite films, as examined by FTIR, is shown in Fig. 4 a. Absorption peaks at wave number 1731 cm−1 can be attributed to stretching vibration caused by –CO in PMMA [40]. With the increase of CPDs loading, the crystalline phase transition of PVDF in the composites occurs from α phase to β phase, and this phenomenon is manifested by the weakening of the intensity of the characteristic peaks of the α phase at 764 and 975 cm−1, while the intensity of the characteristic peaks of the β phase at 840, 1179 and 1403 cm−1 is enhanced [41].

Subsequently, based on the further analysis of the FTIR, it is found that the β phase content of all the composite films increases by about 10 % (Fig. S1a), which may be due to the weak dipole-dipole interaction between CPDs and PVDF. Fig. 4b presents the crystalline structure of the composite films using XRD profiles. The peaks at 18.7°, 20.2° and 20.8° represent the reflections of (020)α-PVDF, (110)α-PVDF and (110)/(200)β-PVDF, respectively. It has been proven in many previous studies that the size of polar β crystallite affects the polarization behavior of composites [42]. The results in Fig. S1b demonstrate that the introduction of CPDs does not significantly affect the size of β crystallites in the composites, which is maintained at ∼7.5 nm. As the DSC results showing in Fig. 4c, all the prepared polymer-based CPDs composite films show a single melting temperature (
T m
) of ∼157 °C, indicating that the introduction of CPDs do not result in thicker lamellae of PVDF. As shown in Fig. S1c, the crystallinity of polymer composite blends always maintains at approximately 47 %. In addition, according to Fig. 4c, the introduction of CPDs has a remarkable effect on the glass transition temperature (
T g
) of the PVDF/PMMA blend. In the DSC measurement temperature range, the PVDF/PMMA blend exhibits a single
T
g 1
near 60 °C, while the composites containing CPDs show a new
T
g 2
near 90 °C, implying that the introduction of CPDs leads to concentration fluctuations in amorphous regions within the blend. When the loadings of CPDs are 0.02, 0.05 and 0.1 wt%, respectively, there are obvious
T
g 1
peaks and negligible
T
g 2
peaks in their respective DSC curves. However, when the loadings of CPDs reach to high loadings of 0.5 and 1 wt%, the
T
g 1
peaks disappear. while the
T
g 2
peaks dominate.

TMDSC can superimpose a sine modulation waveform on the basis of traditional DSC linear temperature rise to separate reversible and irreversible heat flow, and the measurement of reversible heat flow has been widely used in the study of
T g
of polymer blends. Herein, the TMDSC results for the composites loading with CPDs are plotted as the reversible heat capacity and the derivative of the reversible heat capacity, respectively, as given in Fig. 5 a and b. Firstly, only one step-like glass transition temperature can be observed in all the curves, indicating that the PVDF and PMMA are miscible as reported before. The midpoint of the peaks in the curve of reversible heat capacity is labeled as
T
g , m i d
. The
T
g , m i d
of PVDF/PMMA blend, composites with 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 wt% loadings of CPDs are 53.5, 56.6, 58.2, 60.5, 64.1 and 64.7 °C, respectively, exhibiting a gradual increase. The increase in
T
g , m i d
usually reveals the suppressed molecular mobility owing to the interaction originated from CPDs rich in amino groups and polymers. For example, the amino groups in CPDs could be preferred to create hydrogen bonding associations with the carbonyl groups in PMMA chains. Besides, the geometric constraints originated from ultrasmall sized CPDs may intensify the interactions between CPDs and polymers. It is also found that a broad
T g
is observed for all the blends containing CPDs, which probably indicates the presence of dynamic heterogeneity in the amorphous phase owing to the different interaction between CPDs with polymer component [43,44]. According to the Donth fluctuation formula [44], the cooperative volume (
ξ
) near
T g
was obtained for all the blends as shown in Table S1. This clearly suggests that the cooperative volume (
ξ
) as well as the number of segments cooperatively relaxing is reduced due to specific preferred interaction of PMMA with CPDs. It should be noted that although CPDs is assumed to be preferred to interact with PMMA, whereas the amino groups are also confirmed to form dipole-dipole interaction with PVDF. We further investigated the broad
T g
via the derivative curves derived from the corresponding heating capacity vs. temperature curves. As shown in Fig. 5b, the pure PVDF/PMMA blend exhibits a single
T
g , m i d
at 53.5 °C, but the
T
g , m i d
of the composites with the loading of CPDs is divided into two, one locating at ∼50 °C and the other at ∼70 °C. It is assumed that affected by the interfacial confinement of CPDs, the gradient of segment motility is expanding, i.e., the molecular chain segments near CPDs have different motility than those far from CPDs [45]. The
T
g 1
near 50 °C is possibly produced by molecular chains away from the surface of CPDs, while the
T
g 2
near 70 °C comes from molecular chains with higher entanglement density adsorbed on the surface of uniformly dispersed CPDs. Increased entanglement density near nanodots is favourable for energy storage and breakdown in dielectric polymers [46,47].

The relaxation behavior of the composite films is analyzed through the curve of tan δ vs. temperature in Fig. 5c. The peak at 20–40 °C is attributable to the relaxation of amorphous region between lamellae (
α c
), while the broad peak at 60–90 °C can be attributed to the merging of the amorphous region (
α m
) relaxation and
α β
relaxation of PMMA. It can be observed that as for the CPDs/PVDF/PMMA blend, both the temperatures shift to higher ones, confirming the location of CPDs in amorphous region and the confinement around CPDs for polymer chains. For comparison, the TMDSC curves of the low molecular weight PMMA composite film was obtained (Figs. S2a and b), which show the same characteristics as the high molecular weight PMMA composite film. Based on the crystallization behaviors, the glass transition and structural relaxation behaviors (Fig. 5), we envisage that the CPDs can be mainly introduced into the amorphous phase of PVDF/PMMA blends. Because the CPDs have no significant effect on the crystallization behaviors of PVDF, the variation of
T g
in the amorphous region indicates the concentration fluctuations accompanied with formation of a percolated domain of PVDF/PMMA interphase around the surface of CPDs. Upon increase of CPDs loading, the volume fraction of interfacial region gradually enlarged. As a result, the interface enriched region exhibits a “glassy” behavior with slower relaxation dynamics compared to the bulk polymer, which significantly improved the “rigidity” of amorphous region. With continuations growth of CPDs loading, the “glassy” interfacial domains start to overlap with neighbors, giving rise to the formation of penetrating interphase with increased molecular entanglement. Herein, the ultrasmall sized CPDs serve as physical cross-linkers in the amorphous region, which generates an increased molecular entanglement via hydrogen bonding, dipole-dipole interaction etc. physical interaction that interpenetrates multiple chains of both PVDF and PMMA. Such effect of CPDs has also been reported by Wu et al. [48], and they have achieved strong yet tough diene-rubber by incorporating amine-passive carbon nanodots as cross-linkers to engineer multiple sacrificial units into a chemically cross-linked network. In other words, CPDs tend to adsorb molecular chains in amorphous regions of the PVDF/PMMA blend, which in turn increases the entanglement of molecular chains in the amorphous region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dielectric performance of polymer-based CPDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to explore the effect of the introduction of CPDs on the dielectric properties of the as-prepared composites, the dependence of AC conductivity (
σ
),
ε r
and dielectric loss (
tan
δ
) on the electric field frequency at room temperature are illustrated, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . In Fig. 6a, the
σ
of pure PVDF/PMMA blend and all composites loaded with CPDs show a good frequency dependence, which is the direct evidence of their excellent insulating property. In the range of 101–103 Hz, the relaxation of interfacial polarization formed between CPDs and polymer matrix occurs thus brings about the decline of
ε r
and
tan
δ
. When the electric field frequency reaches 105–107 Hz, the relaxation of dipole polarization occurs and leads to the decrease of
ε r
, moreover, the violent friction between dipoles causing the drastic increase of
tan
δ
(Fig. 6b and c). The corresponding
ε r
and
tan
δ
at a frequency of 103 Hz are extracted from the BDS curves and are plotted as Fig. 6d. The
ε r
(@ 103 Hz) of the PVDF/PMMA blend, and composites with 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 wt% CPDs are 8.1, 7.9, 7.8, 7.5, 7.1 and 6.9, respectively, while the
tan
δ
shows a minimum value of ∼0.009 when the loading of CPDs is 0.1 wt%. The introduction of CPDs effectively increases the molecular chain entanglement density in amorphous regions of PVDF/PMMA blend, therefore limiting the dipole orientation under the electric field, resulting in a slight decline in the
ε r
of the blend, while the
tan
δ
is significantly suppressed at a low level. On the other hand, the inorganic carbon core that trapped an electron will generate an electrostatic energy with value of
e 2
2 C
and thus act as scattering centers for electrons within the PVDF/PMMA blend [45]. However, when the loading of CPDs exceeds 0.1 wt%, the proximity or even agglomeration between fillers reduces the potential barrier for electron transport, resulting in the deterioration of the
tan
δ
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Breakdown behavior of polymer-based CPDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The Weibull distribution of breakdown strength is plotted in Fig. 7 a. The fitted characteristic breakdown strength
E b
is calculated by using the following equation [47]:
(4)
P
( E )
= 1 − exp
(
−
(
E
E b
)
β
)
where
P
( E )
is the cumulative probability of electric breakdown,
E
is the actual measured breakdown strength of the samples,
E b
is the characteristic breakdown strength and
β
is the reliability parameter. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, the
E b
of the pure PVDF/PMMA blend, and the composites with 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 wt% CPDs are 507.8, 533.8, 607.9, 652.0, 584.1, 512.9 MV/m, respectively. In particular, the
E b
value and the
β
value of the composites reach the maximum when the loading of CPDs is 0.1 wt%, which indicates the enhanced breakdown characteristic of the blend. In general, the highly ordered molecular chains in crystalline regions of the blend can better resist carrier transport, while the random molecular chains in amorphous regions will become leakage channels. In order to verify the maximum of
E b
, more loading concentration (0.08 wt% and 0.13 wt%) of CPDs have introduced into polymer blends, and it is found that the composite film with a filler content of 0.1 wt% is still the maximum value as shown in Fig. S3. In order to further explore the underlying mechanism of high
E b
, the carrier trapping ability of the composite films is measured by thermally stimulated depolarising current (TSDC) and the results are shown in Fig. 7c. For the pure PVDF/PMMA blend film curve, there is a clear peak near 70 °C. For the composite film, a significantly higher peak near 70 °C corresponds to more carrier escape. It means that more traps are introduced in the composite film in order that more carriers could be captured, which is well matched with the improved
E b
. Combined with the microscopic structure, the diagram of carrier migration has shown in Fig. 7d, the electrons under the external electric field will cross the molecular chains in amorphous regions more easily and eventually lead to breakdown of the blend. The mechanism of CPDs reinforcing amorphous regions of the PVDF/PMMA blend is shown in Fig. 7e. As it is observed, the introduction of CPDs results in higher
T g
in the PVDF/PMMA blend, which means a decrease in free volume and increased entanglement, thus compressing the electron transport path and preventing it from obtaining sufficient kinetic energy. Zhang et al. have presented a blending strategy to induce more extended chain packing density and decreased free volume with increased chain entanglement, resulting in a significantly enhanced breakdown strength of 1000 MV/m [49]. Therefore, the reinforcement effect of CPDs in amorphous region could be responsible for the highly promoted
E b
of PVDF/PMMA. On the other hand, the Coulomb blockade effect of CPDs complicates the growth paths within the blend of electrical trees and theoretically contribute to the enhancement of
E b
. We also investigated the case of blend of PVDF with lower molecular weight of PMMA. As shown in Fig. S2, it can be found that the crystallization, glass transition and structural relaxation behaviors (Fig. S2a, b and c) of the CPDs/PVDF/PMMA blend composites remain unchanged compared to PVDF/PMMA. Nevertheless, the Coulomb blockade effect of CPDs makes the
E b
of PVDF/PMMA be increased by only near 20 % (Fig. S2e). Moreover, the high molecular weight has a higher breakdown strength than the low molecular weight PMMA blend composite films because of the physical entanglement density between the polymers increases as the molecular weight increases. Therefore, it can be inferred that both the introduction of CD and high molecular weight PMMA have comparable impacts. Furthermore, the CPDs induced microstructure evolution in amorphous phase is mainly responsible for the significantly increase of
E b
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy storage performance of polymer-based CPDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to characterize the magnitude of leakage currents generated under different electric fields inside the dielectric composite films containing different CPDs loadings, the I-E curves of the pure PVDF/PMMA blend and composites were tested under 0–100 MV/m, the results are shown in Fig. 8 a. Due to the reduction of energy dissipated in the form of Joule heat, the composites loaded with CPDs obtain the lower
tan
δ
. The charging and discharging behavior of dielectric composite films under high electric fields can be recorded by D-E loops shown in Fig. S4. Fig. 8b shows the D-E loops of the composite films loaded with different contents of CPDs at each electric field and the corresponding D-E loops at the maximum electric field, respectively. In fact, it is not difficult to find that the introduction of a moderate amount of CPDs reduces the conductivity loss and hysteresis loss of the PVDF/PMMA blend, making the D-E curves more slender. The value of net polarization (
D m
−
D r
) is also crucial for the energy storage of the dielectric composites, where the
D m
and
D r
are the maximum polarization and remanent polarization, respectively. The
D m
−
D r
values of PVDF/PMMA blend, and the composites with 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 wt% loadings of CPDs are 2.8, 3.36, 3.36, 4.26, 3.68 and 3.07 μC/cm2, respectively (Fig. 8c). It is worth mentioning that, by comparing Fig. S3, Fig. 8b and c, the introduction of CPDs in fact does not contribute significantly to the polarizability of the PVDF/PMMA blend, while the enhancement of
D m
−
D r
values only comes from the significant improvement of the
E b
. The
U d
can be obtained by integrating the D-E loops in Fig. S3, the equation used therein are as follows:
(5)
U d
=
∫
D r
D m
E d D =
∫
D r
D m
ε 0
ε r
E d E
Correspondingly, the
η
can be calculated by using the following equation:
(6)
η =
U d
U
× 100 %

The
U d
and the
η
of the PVDF/PMMA blend and the composites under each electric field are plotted in Fig. 8d. The increase in electric field brings higher
U d
to the composites, but the leakage current and hysteresis loss cause a deterioration in
η
. Among them, the composite loading with 0.1 wt% CPDs obtains a superior
U d
of 12.4 J/cm3 at 650 MV/m, which is 118 % higher than that of the PVDF/PMMA blend (5.7 J/cm3). Meanwhile, it is pleasing to see that the
η
at this point still remains at ∼70 %. In addition, compared with other typical PVDF-based composites [50–59], this work shows remarkable improvements as shown in Fig. 9 . This improvement is mainly because the new strategy endows the PVDF matrix with significantly promotion of breakdown strength. This further proves that this effective strategy can be considered to prepare electrically insulating materials with resistance to electrical breakdown.

In conclusion, the CPDs prepared in this work have a unique organic/inorganic hybrid structure, which can act as a bond between the polymer and the inorganic filler to enhance the performance of polymer-based dielectrics. In addition, due to the crystallization induced phase separation, the PMMA molecular chains as well as the CPDS are further expelled into the amorphous region of the PVDF, which results in an elevation of the PMMA concentration in the amorphous zone, consequently augmenting the physical entanglement density. It is confirmed that, the introduction of CPDs does not significantly affect the crystallization behavior and crystalline structure of the PVDF/PMMA blend. However, benefiting from the retention of organic polymer chains in CPDs, the
T g
of the blend is greatly increased, which implies an enhancement in the density of molecular chain entanglement in amorphous regions of the blend. Therefore, the introduction of CPDs effectively improves the
E b
of PVDF/PMMA blend, which in turn significantly enhances their dielectric energy storage behaviors. In particular, the composite loaded with 0.1 wt% CPDs exhibits the superior
U d
of 12.4 J/cm3 at the
E b
of 652.0 MV/m.
